title: Applications of Gauss's Law
summary: Charge Q will be distributed over the surface of hollow metal sphere.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - $$ \phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$
Electric field due to line charge (1), $$ \overrightarrow{E}_{1}=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}R}\quad i\ N/C $$ Electric field due to line charge (2), $$ \overset{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{E}_{2}}=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\mathrm{N/C} $$ $$ \begin{align*}\mathrm{E}_{net}^{\rightarrow}&=\mathrm{E}_{1}^{\rightarrow}\mathrm{E}_{2}^{\rightarrow}\\&=\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}+\frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\\&=\frac{\lambda}{\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}}\hat{i}\quad\mathrm{N/C}.\end{align*} $$ - Charge Q will be distributed over the surface of hollow metal sphere.
(i) For $ r < R $ (inside), By gauss law, $$ \begin{array}{c}\oint\limits_{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{in}}}\rightarrow\rightarrow\rightarrow\rightarrow d\mathrm{s}=\frac{q_{e n}}{\varepsilon_{0}}\\\Rightarrow\quad\mathrm{E}_{i n}=0\end{array} $$ $$ (\because q_{en}=0) $$ (ii) For $ r > R $ (outside)
$$ \oint\overrightarrow{E_{\mathrm{o u t}}}\cdot\overrightarrow{d s}=\frac{q_{e n}}{e_{0}} $$ Here $$ q_{en}=Q $$ $$ \because q_{en}=Q) $$ $$ \mathrm{For}r>\mathrm{R},\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{out}}\xrightarrow{\rightarrow}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\left|\vec{r}^{2}\right|}\hat{r} $$ $$ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{out}}\propto\frac{1}{r^{2}} $$ $ \Rightarrow $ $ E_{out} $ decreases. ##### △! Caution While applying the Gauss law over a Gaussian surface, students should not think that the calculated electric field is only due to the charges inside the Gaussian surface. It is the resultant electric field of all the charges, present there.

title: Continuous Charge Distribution
summary: After connecting the spheres by a conducting wire, they will be at same potential in equilibrium condition.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Total charge on cube $q = 6\ \mu\mathrm{C}$ Side of cube, $a = 0.05\ m$ As cube has 6 square forces, Surface area = $6a^{2}$ Surface charge density $=\frac{\text{Total Charge}}{\text{Surface area}}$ $=\frac{q}{6a^{2}}$ $=\frac{6\times10^{-6}\mathrm{C}}{6\times(0.05)^{2}}$ $=0.4\times10^{-3}\mathrm{C}/\mathrm{m}^{2}$ - Given: $ r = 10 \, \text{cm} = 10 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m} $ Charge, $ q = 3.2 \times 10^{-7} \, C $ ∴ Electric field at a point (x = 15 cm) from centre of sphere is: $$ \begin{aligned}E&=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q}{x^{2}}\\&=9\times10^{9}\times\frac{3.2\times10^{-7}}{225\times10^{-4}}\\&=1.28\times10^{5}N/C\end{aligned} $$ -
Electric field due to the given charged ring is zero at centre O. So, electric field due to AKB is equal and opposite to electric field due to ACBD, from the principle of superposition. Since, E is electric field strength, due to AKB of O along $ \overrightarrow{KO} $. So, electric field strength due to ACDB along $ \overrightarrow{OK} $ and it is equal to $ \overrightarrow{E} $. - Given, $ E_{\text{max}} = 3 \times 10^6 \, \text{V/m} $ and $ R = 3 \, \text{m} $ We have, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\times\frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{R}^{2}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{c c}{\Rightarrow}&{\mathrm{Q_{m a x}}=4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}^{2}\mathrm{E_{m a x}}}\\ {}&{=\frac{3\times3\times3\times10^{6}}{9\times10^{9}}}\\ {}&{=3\times10^{-3}\mathrm{C}}\\ \end{array} $$ - After connecting the spheres by a conducting wire, they will be at same potential in equilibrium condition. ⇔ $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{V}_{1}&=\mathbf{V}_{2}\\\mathbf{K}\frac{q_{1}}{r_{1}}&=\mathbf{K}\frac{q_{2}}{r_{2}}\\\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}&=\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\end{aligned} $$ The ratio of the magnitude of electric fields, $$ \begin{array}{r l r}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~K}q_{1}}{r_{1}^{2}}}{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~K}q_{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{q_{1}}{q_{2}}\bigg(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\bigg)^{2}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{r_{1}}{r_{2}}\bigg(\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}\bigg)^{2}=\frac{r_{2}}{r_{1}}}\\ &{\Rightarrow\quad}&{\frac{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{1}}{\mathrm{\bf~E}_{2}}\;=\;\frac{2}{1}}\end{array} $$

title: Coulomb's Law
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Here, gravitational attractive force between two H-atoms is: $$ \mathrm{F_{G}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}m^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ and electrostatic repulsive force between two H-atom is, $$ \mathrm{F}_{e}=\frac{\mathrm{K}q^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{K}(\Delta e)^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ $\because$ Net charge on H-atom $=(e+\Delta e)-e=\Delta e$ Then we have, $$ \frac{\mathrm{K}(\Delta e)^{2}}{d^{2}}=\frac{\mathrm{G}m^{2}}{d^{2}} $$ $$ \begin{array}{r} \left[\begin{array}{c} 9\times10^{9}\left(\Delta e\right)^{2}=6.67\times10^{-11}\times1.67\times10^{-27}\times1.67 \\ \times10^{-27} \end{array} \right] \\ \times \left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 16 \end{array} \right] \\ \times \left[\begin{array}{cc} 15 & 16 \end{array} \right] \\ \succeq 0 \end{array} $$ $$ (\Delta e)^{2}=\frac{6.67\times1.67\times1.67}{9}\times10^{-74} $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ $$ \Delta e=1.437\times10^{-37}C $$ -
From the diagram, $$ \tan\theta=\frac{F_{e}}{mg}=\theta $$ ### 1.7. Electric Field 10. An electron falls from rest through a vertical distance $h$ in a uniform and vertically upward directed electric field E. The direction of electric field is now reversed, keeping its magnitude the same. A proton is allowed to fall from rest in it through the same vertical distance $h$. The time of fall of the electron, in comparison to the time of fall of the proton is: (A) 10 times greater (B) 5 times greater (C) smaller (D) equal [NEET 2018] 11. The electric field in a certain region is acting radially outward and is given by, E = Ar. A charge contained in a sphere of radius 'a' centered at the origin of the field will be given by: (A) $ 4\pi\varepsilon_{0}Aa^{2} $ (B) $ A\varepsilon_{0}a^{2} $ (C) $ 4\pi\varepsilon_{0}Aa^{3} $ (D) $ \varepsilon_{0}Aa^{3} $ 12. The electric field at a distance $ \frac{3R}{2} $ from the centre of a charged conducting spherical shell of radius R is E. The electric field at a distance $ \frac{R}{2} $ from the centre of the sphere is: (A) zero (B) E (C) $ \frac{E}{2} $ (D) $ \frac{E}{2} $ [AIPMT Mains 2010] 13. A particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is placed at rest in a uniform electric field E and then released. The kinetic energy attained by the particle after moving a distance $y$ is: (A) $qEy^{2}$ (B) $qE^{2}y$ (C) $qEy$ (D) $q^{2}Ey$ [AIPMT 1998] ### 1.9. Electric Flux 14. A square surface of side L metre in the plane of the paper is placed in a uniform electric field E (volt/m) acting along the same plane at an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal side of the square as shown in figure. The electric flux linked to the surface in unit of V·m, is:
(A) EL^{2} (C) $ EL^{2}\sin\theta $ (B) $ EL^{2}\cos\theta $ (D) 0 [AIPMT Screening 2010] 15. A square surface of side L metres is in the plane of the paper. A uniform electric field E (volt/m), also in the plane of the paper, is limited only to the lower half of the square surface (see figure). The electric flux in SI units associated with the surface is:
(B) $ \frac{EL^{2}}{2\varepsilon_{0}} $ $$ EL^{2} $$ (C) $ \frac{EL^{2}}{2} $ (D) zero [AIPMT 2006] ### 1.10. Electric Dipole 16. Two point charges $ -q $ and $ +q $ are placed at a distance of $ L $, as shown in the figure. The magnitude of electric field intensity at a distance $ R $ ( $ R \gg L $) varies as: (A) $ \frac{1}{R^{2}} $ (B) $ \frac{1}{R^{3}} $ (C) $ \frac{1}{R^{4}} $ (D) $ \frac{1}{R^{6}} $ [NEET 2022] 17. Polar molecules are the molecules: (A) having zero dipole moment. (B) acquire a dipole moment only in the presence of electric field due to displacement of charges. (C) acquire a dipole moment only when magnetic field is absent. (D) having a permanent field (D) having a permanent electric dipole moment. [NEET 2021] 18. The electric field at a point on the equatorial plane at a distance r from the centre of a dipole having dipole moment P is given by $(r >> \text{separation of two charges forming the dipole}, \varepsilon_{0} = \text{permittivity of free space})$: (A) $\frac{-\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{3}}$ (B) $\frac{2\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{3}}$ (C) $-\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}$ (D) $\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}r^{2}}$ [NEET 2020] 19. Three point charges +q, -2q and +q are placed at points $ (x = 0, y = a, z = 0) $, $ (x = 0, y = 0, z = 0) $ and $ (x = a, y = 0, z = 0) $ respectively. The magnitude and direction of the electric dipole moment vector of this charge assembly are: (A) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along the line joining points $ (x=0,y=0,z=0) $ and $ (x=a,y=a,z=0) $ (B) qa along the line joining points $ (x=0,y=0,z=0) $ and $ (x=a,y=a,z=0) $ (C) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along +x-direction (D) $ \sqrt{2}qa $ along +y-direction ∴ $$ \frac{Kq^{2}}{x^{2}mg}=\frac{x}{2l} $$ or $$ x^{3}\propto q^{2} $$ or $$ x^{3/2}\propto q $$ Differentiate eq. (i) w.r.t. to time, we get $$ 3x^{2}\frac{dx}{dt}\propto2q\frac{dq}{dt} $$ but $ \frac{dq}{dt} $ is constant. So. $$ x^{2}(v)\propto q $$ Now, replace q from eq. (ii), we get $$ x^{2}\left(\mathrm{v}\right)\propto x^{3/2} $$ $$ \Downarrow: $$ $$ \textbf{v}\propto x^{\frac{-1}{2}} $$ -
$$ \begin{aligned}&Here,\quad&\tan\theta&=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{mg}\\&\Rightarrow&\frac{\frac{r}{2}}{y}&=\frac{kq^{2}}{r^{2}mg}\\&\Rightarrow&y&\propto r^{3}\\&Therefore,\left(\frac{r^{\prime}}{r}\right)^{3}&=\frac{y/2}{y}\\&\Rightarrow&r^{\prime}&=r\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1/3}\end{aligned} $$ - According to the question, $$ \begin{array}{l}\mathrm{Q}\xrightarrow[\mathrm{A}]\quad\frac{q}{r}\\\longleftarrow\quad\mathrm{C}\\\longrightarrow r\end{array}\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{Q}\\\mathrm{B}\end{array} $$ for equilibrium, net force on charge Q=0, $$ \therefore\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{Qq}{\left(\frac{r}{2}\right)^{2}}+\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{Q^{2}}{r^{2}}=0 $$ $$ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{Q^{2}}{r^{2}}=-\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{4Qq}{r^{2}} $$ $$ \mathbf{Q}=-4\mathbf{q} $$ $$ q=-\frac{Q}{4} $$ -
The force of repulsion between them is, $$ \mathrm{F}=k\frac{q q}{d^{2}}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q q}{d^{2}} $$ Here $$ q=ne $$ $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{(n e)(n e)}{d^{2}} $$ $$ n=\sqrt{\frac{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}F d^{2}}{e^{2}}} $$ - We know that, $ F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q^{2}}{r^{2}} $ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$ \varepsilon_{0}=\frac{1}{4\pi}\frac{q^{2}}{Fr^{2}}=\frac{C^{2}}{N-m^{2}} $$ - The charge on hydrogen nucleus, $q_{1}=+e$ ∴ Coulomb's force, $$ \begin{array}{l}\text{F}=\text{K}\frac{\text{QQ}}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\\ =\text{K}\frac{(+e)(-e)}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\\ \quad=-\frac{\text{K}e^{2}}{r^{2}}\hat{r}\end{array} $$ - According to Coulomb's law, force between two charges is directly proportional to the product of charges and inversely proportional to the square of distance between them. $$ \mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r^{2}} $$ where, $ \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}} = \text{proportionality constant} $. If a dielectric medium of constant K is placed between them, then new force between the charges will be, $$ \mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{K}}\frac{q_{1}q_{2}}{r^{2}} $$ Dividing eq. (ii) by (i), we get, $$ \frac{\mathbf{F}^{\prime}}{\mathbf{F}}=\frac{1}{\mathbf{K}} $$ Or $$ \mathrm{F}^{\prime}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{K}} $$ Thus, new force decreases K times. - A pair of equal and opposite point charges separated by a small distance form an electric dipole. Electric dipoles are atoms or molecules of ammonia, water, alcohol, carbon dioxide, HCl and other compounds in which the centres of positive and negative charge distributions are separated by a small distance. 21. C) On the axial line of an electric dipole, the field intensity is given by, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{2p}{r^{3}} $$ Electric field at an equatorial position, $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}}\cdot\frac{p}{r^{3}} $$ where, $p$ is electric dipole moment. From eq. (i) and (ii), we get, $$ E\propto\frac{1}{r^{3}} $$

formulas.latex: (r >> \text{separation of two charges forming the dipole}, \varepsilon_{0} = \text{permittivity of free space})

subtopic: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
title: Dipole in a Uniform External Field
summary: Given: Angle, $ \theta = 30^{\circ} $ Electric Field intensity, $$ E=2\times10^{5}NC^{-1} $$ Dipole length, = 2 cm = 0.02 m $ \tau = pE \sin \theta $ $ = (ql) E \sin \theta $ $ \therefore $ $$…
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Given: Angle, $ \theta = 30^{\circ} $ Electric Field intensity, $$ E=2\times10^{5}NC^{-1} $$ Dipole length, = 2 cm = 0.02 m $ \tau = pE \sin \theta $ $ = (ql) E \sin \theta $ $ \therefore $ $$ \begin{array}{l}q=\displaystyle \frac{\tau}{\text{El}\sin\theta} \\ \qquad = \frac{4}{2 \times 10^{5} \times 0.02 \times \frac{1}{2}} \\ \qquad = 2 \text{ mC} \end{array} $$
##### Related Theory When a dipole is placed in a uniform external electric field, then net force $ (+qE\text{ and }-qE) $ on it, is zero, but it experiences a torque.

title: Gauss's Law
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - If $\oint_{s} \overrightarrow{E} \cdot ds = 0$, then over a surface, the number of flux lines entering the surface must be equal to the number of flux lines exiting from the surface. -
From the figure, it is clear that the sphere encloses a charge $ q = \pm 3 \times 10^{-6} $ C, which means it is a dipole. We know that, $$ \mathrm{Flux},\phi=\frac{(\mathrm{Charge})_{\mathrm{inside}}}{\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{q_{in}}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ Here, total charge inside the sphere is zero. $$ \phi=\frac{0}{\varepsilon_{0}}=0 $$ $$ \bullet \quad \bullet\bullet $$ ∴ Total electric flux is zero. - $$ \phi_{\mathrm{net}}=\frac{\Sigma q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ ∴ Net flux does not depend on size of Gaussian surface. ⇒ So, flux remain unchanged. - Let $\phi_{A}$, $\phi_{B}$ and $\phi_{C}$ be the electric flux linked with A, B and C. ∴ According to Gauss theorem, $$ \phi_{\mathrm{A}}+\phi_{\mathrm{B}}+\phi_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ since $$ \phi_{\mathbf{A}}=\phi_{\mathbf{C}} $$ $$ \circlearrowleft $$ $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}+\phi_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} $$ or $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\phi_{\mathrm{B}} $$ or $$ 2\phi_{\mathrm{A}}={\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}}-\phi $$ {since, $\phi_{B}=\phi_{1}$} $$ \phi_{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{1}{2}\bigg[\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}}-\phi\bigg] $$ - Electric flux through any face, $$ \Phi_{face}=\frac{q}{6\varepsilon_{0}}=\frac{4\pi q}{6(4\pi\varepsilon_{0})} $$ - According to Gauss law, the electric flux through a closed surface is equal to $ \frac{1}{\varepsilon_{0}} $ times the net charge enclosed by the surface. Since, q is the charge enclosed by the surface, then electric flux, \phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_{0}} If charge q is placed in one of the cube's corners, it will be divided into eight similar cubes. Therefore, electric flux through the cube, - According to Gauss's theorem, the total electric flux over a closed surface is $ \frac{1}{E_{0}} $ times the total charge contained inside surface. Total electric flux = $ \frac{\text{Total charge inside cube}}{\varepsilon_{0}} $

title: Objective I
summary: Use a cylindrical Gaussian surface (pill-box) straddling the sheet.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- By Coulomb's law: $F = \dfrac{kq_1q_2}{r^2}$. If $r \to 2r$: $F' = \dfrac{kq_1q_2}{(2r)^2} = \dfrac{kq_1q_2}{4r^2} = \dfrac{F}{4}$. The force decreases by a factor of 4 (inverse-square law). Answer: (D).
- The **electric field** $\vec{E}$ at a point is defined as the force per unit positive test charge placed at that point: $$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{F}}{q_0}$$ It has both **magnitude** ($|\vec{E}|$ in N/C or V/m) and **direction** (the direction of force on a positive charge). It is a vector field. Answer: (C).
- Electric potential $V$ at a point is the work done per unit positive charge in bringing a test charge from infinity to that point: $$V = \frac{W}{q_0} = \frac{kQ}{r}$$ It is a **scalar** — no direction, just a numerical value (positive for positive charges, negative for negative). Unlike the electric field, potentials due to multiple charges add algebraically. Answer: (B).
- The electric field is the **negative gradient** of the electric potential: $$\vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right)$$ In one dimension: $E = -dV/dr$. The negative sign means the field points from high potential to low potential (downhill on the potential "landscape"). Answer: (B).
- Work done by the electric field on charge $q$ moving from A to B: $$W = q(V_A - V_B)$$ If $V_A > V_B$, a positive charge moves spontaneously from A to B (field does positive work). To move a positive charge against the field (B to A), external work $= q(V_B - V_A)$ is needed. Answer: (B).
- An **equipotential surface** is one on which the potential $V$ is constant everywhere. The work done in moving charge $q$ between two points is $W = q(V_A - V_B)$. For any two points on the same equipotential surface, $V_A = V_B$, hence $W = 0$. No work is done regardless of the path taken (as long as the start and end points are on the same equipotential). Answer: (C).
- For an electric dipole with charges $+q$ and $-q$ separated by distance $2a$, the potential at a point on the equatorial plane at distance $r$ from the centre is: $$V = \frac{kq}{r_+} + \frac{k(-q)}{r_-}$$ On the equatorial plane, $r_+ = r_- = \sqrt{r^2 + a^2}$, so $V = 0$. The two contributions cancel exactly. (Note: the electric **field** at the equatorial point is non-zero, but the potential is zero.) Answer: (C).
- In **electrostatic equilibrium**, free charges in a conductor redistribute until they produce an internal field that exactly cancels the external field. Net result: $\vec{E} = 0$ **inside** the conductor. Any excess charge resides on the **surface**; the surface field is $\sigma/\varepsilon_0$ (perpendicular to the surface), but inside it is strictly zero. Answer: (C).
- The centroid of an equilateral triangle of side $a$ is at distance $r = a/\sqrt{3}$ from each vertex. Potential is a scalar: sum the contributions: $$V = \frac{kq}{r} + \frac{k(-q)}{r} + \frac{kq}{r} = \frac{kq}{r} = \frac{kq}{a/\sqrt{3}} = \frac{k q\sqrt{3}}{a}$$ Net: one $+q$ contributes since the $+q$ and $-q$ cancel. Answer: (B).
- For a **uniform electric field** $\vec{E}$ pointing in the $x$-direction, $V = -Ex + $ const. The equipotential surfaces are planes of constant $V$, i.e., planes where $x = $ const — planes **perpendicular to $\vec{E}$**. This is the general result: equipotential surfaces are always perpendicular to field lines. For a point charge the equipotential surfaces are spheres (concentric about the charge). Answer: (C).
- **Gauss's law**: $$\oint_S \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{\rm enc}}{\varepsilon_0}$$ The net electric flux through any closed (Gaussian) surface equals the **total charge enclosed** $Q_{\rm enc}$ divided by the permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0$. Charges outside the surface do not contribute to the net flux (though they do affect the local field). Answer: (A).
- By Gauss's law, total flux through the cube = $Q/\varepsilon_0$. By symmetry the cube has 6 identical faces, and the charge at the centre is equidistant from all faces. Each face subtends the same solid angle at the centre. Therefore flux through each face = $\dfrac{Q}{6\varepsilon_0}$. Answer: (B).
- By Gauss's law with a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r < R$ (inside the shell): $$\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{A} = \frac{Q_{\rm enc}}{\varepsilon_0} = 0 \quad (\text{no charge inside})$$ $$\therefore\; E \cdot 4\pi r^2 = 0 \implies E = 0$$ All charge is on the shell surface; inside there is zero enclosed charge → zero field for all interior points. Answer: (C).
- For $r > R$, choose a spherical Gaussian surface of radius $r$. Enclosed charge = $Q$. $$E\cdot 4\pi r^2 = \frac{Q}{\varepsilon_0} \implies E = \frac{Q}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 r^2} = \frac{kQ}{r^2}$$ A uniformly charged sphere **behaves like a point charge** at all external points. Answer: (A).

formulas.latex: \vec{E} = -\nabla V = -\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial V}{\partial z}\hat{k}\right)

title: Objective II
summary: - **A** ✓: For a point charge, computing the flux through a sphere centred on the charge using Coulomb's law gives $Q/\varepsilon_0$, which is Gauss's law.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- - **A** ✓: If two field lines crossed, the field would have two directions at that point — impossible. - **B** ✓: By convention, field lines start at positive charges (source) and terminate at negative charges (sink), or extend to/from infinity for isolated charges. - **C** ✓: Closer spacing = stronger field; the number of lines through unit area ⊥ to lines represents field strength. - **D** ✗: Electric field lines **cannot** form closed loops — that would imply the electric field is non-conservative, which contradicts $\oint \vec{E}\cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ for static fields. (Magnetic field lines can form loops, not electric.) Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: No field inside — free electrons redistribute until internal field = 0. - **B** ✓: Charges reside on the surface (mutual repulsion drives them outward; inside any Gaussian surface enclosing interior has zero enclosed charge by Gauss's law + $E=0$ inside). - **C** ✓: $\vec{E}$ at the surface must be perpendicular; any tangential component would accelerate surface charges until equilibrium is restored. - **D** ✗: The **entire surface** (and interior) of a conductor in electrostatic equilibrium is at the **same potential** — it is an equipotential body. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: $V = kQ/r > 0$ for $Q > 0$. - **B** ✓: By definition, $V = W_\infty/q_0$. - **C** ✓: $E = -dV/dr$ → $E > 0$ implies $dV/dr < 0$ → $V$ decreases in the direction of $\vec{E}$. - **D** ✓: At the midpoint between equal and opposite charges, $V = kq/r + k(-q)/r = 0$, yet $\vec{E}$ is non-zero (both charges contribute fields in the same direction at the midpoint). Also on the equatorial plane of a dipole: $V = 0$, $E \ne 0$. Answer: (A), (B), (C), (D).
- Gauss's law is *practically* used when there is sufficient symmetry: - **A** ✓: Spherical symmetry → spherical Gaussian surface → field inside solid sphere. - **B** ✓: Cylindrical symmetry → cylindrical Gaussian surface → field of infinite line charge. - **C** ✓: Planar symmetry → pill-box Gaussian surface → uniform field between capacitor plates. - **D** ✗: Without symmetry, $\vec{E}$ cannot be factored out of the flux integral. Gauss's law is still *valid* but not directly **useful** for calculating $\vec{E}$. Answer: (A), (B), (C).
- - **A** ✓: For a point charge, computing the flux through a sphere centred on the charge using Coulomb's law gives $Q/\varepsilon_0$, which is Gauss's law. - **B** ✓: Applying Gauss's law to a spherical surface around a point charge + using spherical symmetry recovers Coulomb's $1/r^2$ law. - **C** ✗: Both laws apply in **any** medium, not just vacuum. In a medium with permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_r\varepsilon_0$, Gauss's law becomes $\oint \vec{D}\cdot d\vec{A} = Q_{\rm free}$ where $\vec{D} = \varepsilon\vec{E}$. - **D** ✓: The fact that flux through any closed surface = const (independent of surface shape) is mathematically equivalent to an inverse-square law in 3D. Answer: (A), (B), (D).
- If $Q_{\rm enc} = 0$, then by Gauss's law $\Phi = Q_{\rm enc}/\varepsilon_0 = 0$. - **A** ✗: $E = 0$ everywhere on the surface is NOT required. External charges can create non-zero fields on the surface; their contributions to the flux cancel out. Only the **net flux** is zero. - **B** ✓: $\Phi = Q_{\rm enc}/\varepsilon_0 = 0$. ✓ - **C** ✗: The interior field is not necessarily zero. External charges create fields inside too. - **D** ✓: $Q_{\rm enc} = 0$ means the total enclosed charge is zero (all positive charges = all negative charges in absolute value, or simply no charges inside). Answer: (B), (D).

title: Overview
summary: Consider force on a charge particle in an electric field is: $$ \mathrm{F}=q\mathrm{E} \tag*{...(i)}$$ And from Newton's second law of motion.
body:
**Key points from solved questions** - Consider force on a charge particle in an electric field is: $$ \mathrm{F}=q\mathrm{E} \tag*{...(i)}$$ And from Newton's second law of motion. $$ F=m a \tag*{...(ii)}$$ From equations (i) and (ii) we get $$ \begin{aligned}qE&=ma\\a&=\frac{qE}{m}\end{aligned} \tag*{...(iii)}$$ Now consider that particle falls from rest means $u=0$ and $s=h$ $$ \begin{array}{c}\therefore\quad\\ s=ut+at^{2}\\ h=\frac{1}{2}at^{2}\\ \Rightarrow\quad h=\frac{1}{2}\bigg(\frac{q\mathrm{E}}{m}\bigg).t^{2}\\ \Rightarrow\quad h=\frac{1}{2}\frac{q\mathrm{E}}{m}t^{2}\\ \Rightarrow\quad t=\sqrt{\frac{2hm}{q - We know that, electric field inside the conductor is zero. So, the electric field at a distance $ \frac{R}{2} $ from the centre of the sphere will also be zero. - We have electric force on charged particle, $$ F=q E $$ Mechanical force on charged particle in a uniform electric field is, $$ F=m a=q E $$ or $$ a=\frac{q E}{m} $$ From the third equation of motion, we get, $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=u^{2}+2a y $$ $$ \mathbf{v}^{2}=\frac{2q\mathrm{E}y}{m} \tag*{...(ii)}$$ $$ \left[u=0\text{and}a=\frac{qE}{m}\right] $$ Now, kinetic energy of the particle, $$ \begin{aligned}\mathbf{K}&=\frac{1}{2}\boldsymbol{m}\mathbf{v}^{2}\\&=\frac{m}{2}\mathbf{\Omega}\times\frac{2q\mathbf{E}\mathbf{y}}{m}=q\mathbf{E}\mathbf{y}\end{aligned} $$ - We know that, $$ \mathrm{f l u x,}\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\phi}=\stackrel{\rightarrow}{\mathrm{E.A}} $$ and $$ =EA\cos\theta $$ $$ \begin{array}{l}\to\to\\ E.A=0,\end{array} $$ Since $\theta = 90^{\circ}$ as $\overrightarrow{A}$ is normal to the plane of square. #### Related Theory The number of electric field lines crossing a surface normally is known as the electric flux. - Electric field due to a dipole at any arbitrary point (R, Q) is $$ \mathrm{E}=\frac{p}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}\mathrm{R}^{3}}\sqrt{3\cos^{2}\theta+1} $$ Here, $$ \mathrm{E}\propto\frac{1}{\mathrm{R}^{3}}. $$ - Polar molecules are the molecules having a permanent electric dipole moment.
##### Related Theory A polar molecule is one, in which the centre of gravity for positive and negative charges are separated. - Electric field due to electric dipole at an equatorial plane at a distance r from the centre of dipole is given by: $$ \mathrm{E}_{e}=\frac{q(2a)}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(r^{2}+a^{2})^{3/2}} $$
Directed from Q to R. $$ \mathrm{E}_{e}=-\frac{\mathrm{P}}{4\pi\varepsilon_{0}(r^{2}+a^{2})^{\frac{3}{2}}} $$ For a very short dipole $(a< ∴ The resultant moment = $ \sqrt{q^{2}a^{2}+q^{2}a^{2}} $ $ =\sqrt{2}qa $ along the direction $45^{\circ}$ that is along OP, where P is $(a,a,0)$.

title: reNEET2026
summary: The point charge in the cavity produces a field there, so $E_A\neq0$.
body:
**Key points from solved questions**
- The point charge in the cavity produces a field there, so $E_A\neq0$. Outside the conductor the field is that of a point charge Q at the sphere's centre (spherically symmetric), so $E_B=E_C$ at equal distances.
